text.title: Python Micro-Benchmarking and Profiling
text.summary: pytest-benchmark for function-level comparisons, py-spy for low-overhead sampling profiling, and SQLAlchemy event hooks for slow-query detection.

rule.statement: Use pytest-benchmark for comparing two implementations (e.g., orjson vs json, sync vs async ORM). Require at least 3 rounds and report mean ± stdev. Never accept a single-run result as evidence.
rule.rationale: Single runs include JIT warmup, OS scheduling noise, and cache effects. Statistical rounds filter transient outliers.

rule.statement: Use py-spy record (sampling profiler, no instrumentation) for profiling live services. Never run cProfile (deterministic, 10-30% overhead) simultaneously with a load test.
rule.rationale: Deterministic profilers change the timing they measure. py-spy attaches to a running PID with negligible overhead and produces flame graphs from real load.

example.code:
# Record flame graph for running process py-spy record -o profile.svg --pid <PID> --duration 60
# Live top-like view py-spy top --pid <PID>
# View SVG in browser (or use speedscope)

rule.statement: Log every SQL query that exceeds 100 ms. Detect N+1 patterns by counting queries per request in tests and asserting the count is below a threshold (e.g., < 5 queries for a list of 10 items).
rule.rationale: N+1 queries are the most common source of latency spikes and invisible to request-level metrics unless explicitly counted.
